finding.message: This path depends on a [user-provided value](1).

answers: The exact file-operation sink is `target.write_bytes(request.body)` on line 142. The path value passed to it is `target`, which is derived from `BASE_EXPORT_DIR / name`.
The assignments to the path value are: line 139: `name = request.GET.get("name", "packet.bin")`, line 140: `target = BASE_EXPORT_DIR / name`. The last assignment is on line 140.

The value of `target` derives from `name`, which is assigned on line 139 as `request.GET.get("name", "packet.bin")`. This directly retrieves user input from the GET parameters. The data flow is: `request` (line 136) → `name` (line 139) → `target` (line 140) → sink (line 142).
There are no constant-time conditions or reassignments that override the user-tainted value. No defensive checks or sanitizations are present in the code snippet.

There is no specific defense mechanism applied to the path. No use of `secure_filename`, `resolve()`, `is_relative_to()`, or any other path sanitization or validation technique is visible. The additional context does not reveal any protections in `BASE_EXPORT_DIR` or the caller context.

The user-tainted value `name` from `request.GET` reaches the sink at line 142 without any visible sanitization or validation. The additional context does not provide any evidence of protections, so the vulnerability assessment remains the same.
verification.reasoning: User-controlled input `name` is used to construct a file path which is then written to using `write_bytes`. There is no sanitization or validation of the input, and the additional context does not reveal any protections. This makes it vulnerable to path traversal attacks.
verification.data_flow: request (line 136) → name = request.GET.get("name", ...) (line 139) → target = BASE_EXPORT_DIR / name (line 140) → target.write_bytes(...) (line 142)
